paper_title: Lius: Translation Model Based Instructional Lingustic Using Continual Instruction Tuning In Kupang Malay

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new potential for translation tasks but often experience performance degradation when handling low-resource languages. To address this limitation, we propose an approach for fine-tuning LLMs on a low-resource language, Kupang Malay. Our approach involves designing a set of instructions by leveraging explicit lexical and semantic features from a bilingual dictionary, and introducing Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT), a training paradigm that enables iterative instruction-based training. Experimental results demonstrate that our model, named Lius, yields notable improvements over standard instruction-tuned models by outperforming 4-6 points, and surpassing both Neural Machine Translation (NMT) and Multilingual LLM models by 10-13 points on several evaluation metrics. These findings highlight the potential of our approach to mitigate the reliance on large-scale parallel data in low-resource language translation.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new potential for translation tasks but often experience performance degradation when handling low-resource languages. To address this limitation, we propose an approach for fine-tuning LLMs on lowresource language, Kupang Malay. Our approach involves designing set of instructions by leveraging explicit lexical and semantic features from bilingual dictionary, and introducing Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT), training paradigm that enables iterative instruction-based training. Experimental results demonstrate that our model, named Lius, yields notable improvements over standard instruction-tuned models by outperforming 46 points, and surpassing both Neural Machine Translation (NMT) and Multilingual LLM models by 1013 points on several evaluation metrics. These findings highlight the potential of our approach to mitigate the reliance on largescale parallel data in low-resource language translation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Neural Machine Translation (NMT) represents significant advancement in machine translation by leveraging Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) to model the conditional probability of target sentence given source sentence x. Architectures such as Multilayer LSTM (Sutskever et al., 2014), RNN Encoder-Decoder (Cho et al., 2014), Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), and Multilayer CNN (Gehring et al., 2017) have demonstrated considerable improvements over earlier statistical approaches. However, NMT systems heavily depend on the availability of large-scale parallel corpora, which are predominantly available for highresource languages like English, French, or Chinese. Meanwhile, many languages lack adequate parallel data and are often unsupported by commercial translation systems such as Google Translate (Hedderich et al., 2021; Haddow et al., 2022). The case of Indonesia illustrates this disparity. With over 718 identified local languages, only small subset is represented in NLP resources. For instance, Javanese, despite having over 84 million speakers, is represented by merely 12 million parallel sentence pairs, contrast to Dutch, which has over 400 million in the OPUS (Tiedemann, 2012) corpus. Initiatives such as NusaX and NusaWrites (Cahyawijaya et al., 2023a; Winata et al., 2023) have begun addressing these gaps, yet the collection of parallel data remains time-consuming and resource-intensive. Several challenges, such as shortage of qualified annotators, dialectal variation, and inconsistent orthographic standards (Aji et al., 2022; Novitasari et al., 2020), complicate both model development and parallel data collection efforts. In response, researchers have explored monolingual corpora and bilingual dictionaries as alternatives. Monolingual data, which are more readily available, has been used to support unsupervised or semi-supervised NMT training, demonstrating notable improvements in output quality (Baziotis et al., 2020; Chronopoulou et al., 2021). Bilingual dictionaries have also been utilized to replace rare or low-frequency words during training, leading to enhanced translation performance (Duan et al., 2020; Pourdamghani et al., 2019). Furthermore, multilingual training, transfer learning, and pivot-based strategies (Dabre et al., 2020; Leng et al., 2019; Zoph et al., 2016) provide cross-lingual generalization capabilities that enhance adaptability and robustness, especially for low-resource settings. However, these approaches often lack the fine-grained lexical and semantic mappings available in parallel data and struggle to capture the complex linguistic phenomena in specific languages. The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) has significantly shifted the paradigm in machine translation. Trained on massive and diverse datasets in an unsupervised approach, LLMs exhibit strong generalization capabilities across various NLP tasks. These models are especially attractive for translation tasks as they are not strictly dependent on parallel corpora. Some studies have shown that, even in zero-shot settings, LLMs can perform competitively with traditional NMT systems trained on large parallel datasets (Lyu et al., 2024). Nonetheless, the performance of LLMs tends to degrade when applied to non-English or low-resource languages due to over-reliance on English during pretraining (Robinson et al., 2023; Jiao et al., 2023; Bang et al., 2023). These models often fail to capture the rich lexical and semantic variation present in low-resource languages. To address these limitations, this research adopts LLMs while proposing an Instructional Linguistics approach using Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT). The model will be trained continuously with linguistically informed instructions, incorporating lexical and semantic features from bilingual dictionaries, such as part-of-speech categories, synonyms, antonyms, and grammatical rules. This approach aims to enhance the models understanding of lexical-semantic relations, particularly in low-resource settings where such relations are rarely explicit. As case study, this work focuses on Kupang Malay, Malay-based creole spoken in the western part of Timor Island, which remains underrepresented in digital NLP resources.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Recent studies have investigated various strategies to enhance multilingual translation models, particularly for low-resource languages, including approaches such as instruction tuning and prompt engineering. The following section outlines recent approach implemented to address these challenges. studies Instructiong Tuning Recent have demonstrated that leveraging monolingual and parallel corpora through approaches such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on large language models, including Llama-2 (Touvron et al., 2023), MaLA-500 (Lin et al., 2024), and Mistral (Jiang improvement et al., 2023), enables significant on the low-resource languages task, especially when pretraining is aligned with the linguistic characteristics of the target language (Iyer et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2023). Instruction tuning has also emerged as key technique, where cross-lingual supervision is used to align bilingual or multilingual data in way that enhances generalization across tasks and languages. This has been implemented through frameworks that extract structured instructions auch as word-level alignments using tools like FastAlign (Dyer et al., 2013), and apply them to models such as BLOOMZ (Muennighoff et al., 2023) and XGLM (Lin et al., 2022), achieving measurable improvements in translation quality, often surpassing previous baselines by up to several BLEU points (Cahyawijaya et al., 2023b; Mao and Yu, 2024; Li et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2022; Muennighoff et al., 2023; Dyer et al., 2013). Prompt Engineering Instead of relying on heavy training process, some studies have designed instruction-specific approaches for lowresource language translation, aiming to improve model interpretability and output quality. By combining monolingual corpora, bilingual dictionaries, and syntactic patterns, models like BLOOMZ (Muennighoff et al., 2023) and ChatGPT benefit from reduced ambiguity and improved lexical alignment (Guo et al., 2024; Muennighoff et al., 2023). Similar gains have been observed through the use of semantic embeddings such as LASER (Heffernan et al., 2022) to refine word correspondences in specific languages (Merx et al., 2024; Heffernan et al., 2022). Advances in prompt design, including domain-specific, morphologicallyinformed, and context-aware instructions, have shown to further support model reasoning and translation accuracy across tasks and models like GPT-4 and Mistral (Peng et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2024a; Huang et al., 2023; He et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2023).

sections.title: 3 Kupang Malay Language
sections.content: Kupang Malay is Malay-based creole spoken in the western part of Timor Island and is widely used as lingua franca across various ethnic communities. Although it originated through creolization rather than as native ethnic language, it has evolved into stable and distinct linguistic system (Rafael, 2019). The language exhibits minimal morphological complexity such as limited to four prefixes, with no suffixes or infixes, and vowel system similar to Indonesian, excluding the schwa vowel (Jacob and Grimes, 2003). Several dialects such as Air Mata, Alor Malay, and Basa Kupang reflect the diverse vernacular influences of its speakers. Kupang Malay is classified as low-resource language in the field of natural language processing (NLP). It lacks substantial parallel corpora in major multilingual datasets such as OPUS (Tiedemann, 2012), WikiMatrix, and CCMatrix (Schwenk et al., 2021a,b). Existing resources for Kupang Malay are sparse and include only few datasets such as Taxi1500 (Ma et al., 2024), PanLex (Kamholz et al., 2014), and the Bhinneka Korpus (Lopo and Tanone, 2024), each providing limited data coverage for the language. Despite being spoken by an estimated 5.3 million people in East Nusa Tenggara (NTT), Kupang Malay has yet to receive meaningful technological support. Bridging this gap through NLP development could not only promote digital inclusion for its speakers but also serve as gateway to revitalizing over 70 other underrepresented languages in the region.

sections.title: Instructional Linguistic
sections.content: Instructional Linguistics is an approach that applies linguistic theories and principles to design an instruction to guide the model during training. There are four prompts that have been derived, i.e., Context-based 4.3, Semantic-based 4.4, Phonetic-based 4.5, and List-Group-Labelbased 4.6. Following section will explain in details what is the theory behind this and the background of the created prompts. 4.1 Background In this study, Instructional Linguistics are motivated by Direct Instruction, introduced by Engelmann and Wesley Becker, where learning is delivered explicitly and systematically by the instructor (Zahriani, 2014). It is also aligned with Explicit Instruction, which emphasizes clear learning objectives and reduced cognitive load (Hughes et al., 2017). From linguistic perspective, this approach resonates with Stephen D. Krashens Second Language Acquisition theory, which underscores the importance of comprehensible and sufficiently challenging input in the language learning process (Pauzan, 2024). Figure 1: Illustration of the sentence representation extraction. 4.2 Sentence Representation The process of instruction generation requires sentence representation that serves as proxy for extracting lexical and semantic features relevant to the instruction. We employ the KeyBERT method (Grootendorst, 2021; Sharma and Li, 2019)1 using Indonesian BERT model 2 (Wilie et al., 2020) to identify the most semantically relevant word in sentence. Figure 1 illustrates the process of extraction of sentence representations for an Indonesian sentence. In general, the process begins by obtaining the sentence embedding representation Wid = W1, W2, . . . , Wm, where Wi represents each word in the sentence. The sentence embedding representation EWid is derived from the special [CLS] token output by the BERT model, while the embedding of each word EWi is obtained from its respective token representation. Next, cosine similarity is computed between the document embedding EWid and each word embedding EWi using Equation 1. cos (EWid, EWi) = EWid EWi EWidEWi (1) To determine the most appropriate Wi for each sentence, the word with the highest cosine similarity value is selected as the candidate for sentence representation, as defined in Equation 2. = arg max cos (EWid, EWi) (2) 4.3 Context-Based Prompt The context-based prompt, inspired by the work of Guo et al. (2024) and Merx et al. (2024), incorporates sentence examples in the target language that contain the semantic representation of 1https://github.com/MaartenGr/keyBERT 2https://huggingface.co/indobenchmark/ indobert-large-p2 Figure 2: Illustration of semantic mapping-based instruction. Figure 3: Illustration of phonetic-based instruction construction. the input sentence. Specifically, given an input sentence in Indonesian Sid and its corresponding semantic representation , the equivalent term in Kupang Malay, denoted as mkn, is retrieved using bilingual dictionary. However, if no equivalent mkn is found, the original is retained without translation. Subsequently, the context-based instruction retrieves example sentences from the bilingual dictionary that contain the word mkn. These example senmkn, S2 tences, S1 mkn, serve to reinforce the models understanding of the relationship between the source sentence representation and its contextual realization in the target language. mkn, . . . , Sn 4.4 Semantic Mapping-Based Prompt Semantic-based prompt inspired by the work of Ghazvininejad et al. (2023), which provides bilingual word pairs in the prompt. Firstly, the input Sid is represented by salient word . This word is then searched for the parallel in Kupang Malay as mkn using bilingual dictionary. Next, mkn is mapped to its vector representation E(W mkn) using FastText embeddings (Joulin et al., 2017) trained on monolingual Kupang Malay text. then searches for the nearest neighboring words in Kupang Malay based on cosine similarity between E(W mkn) and all other word vectors E(Wmkn) in the target language. The retrieved nearest neighbors, , are then represented in Kupang Malay. Each of these words is subsequently matched with their Indonesian counterparts using the bilingual dictionary. An illustration of the construction is shown in Figure 2. The model , . . . , , mkn mkn2 mkni 4.5 Phonetic-Based Prompt Phonetic-based prompt enables the model to map sentence representations from the source language to the target language by identifying phonetically similar words, which is expected to improve model performance. This idea is motivated by the work of Atkinson and Raugh (1975) in the pedagogy field. Given Indonesian character ci id at position and the Kupang Malay character ci mkn at the same position, the instruction begins by finding the phonetic representation of the word mkn and applying the phonetic rule (see Equation 3) extracted from the bilingual dictionary. (cid:0)ci id (cid:1) ci mkn i, if ci After the phonetic rule is obtained, the phonetic representation of the sentence mkn is derived as id = ci (3) mkn R(W mkn) = {R(c1), R(c2), . . . , R(cn)} where {c1, c2, . . . , cn} are the characters in the word. To compute the phonetic similarity between mkn and candidate word Wmkn in the target language, sequence matching algorithm3 is used. The similarity score is calculated using Equation 5, where is the length of the longest matching subsequence between R(W mkn) and R(Wmkn). If the similarity score sim(W mkn, Wmkn) τ (with threshold τ = 0.7), the two words are considered phonetically similar. An illustration of construction is shown in Figure 3. = max (len(R(W mkn)), len(R(Wmkn))) (4) sim(W mkn, Wmkn) = (5) 4.6 List-Group-Label-Based Prompt This prompt is inspired by Rekrut (1996), which introduces technical vocabulary through the categorization of sentences or objects within instructional content. The prompt generation begins by 3https://github.com/python/cpython/ blob/3.13/Lib/difflib.py Figure 4: Illustration of List-Group-Label based instruction. identifying list of words related to specific term, then grouping these words and assigning label to each group. 1 , Specifically, given an input sentence represen2 , . . . , tation = , each representation is used to retrieve closest words Wi1, Wi2, . . . , Win in the Kupang Malay language using FastText embeddings. This approach enriches the model with broader understanding of vocabulary and context, especially when learning cross-lingual semantics. The illustration of this technique is illustrated in Figure 4.

sections.title: 5 Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT)
sections.content: Continual Instruction Tuning (CIT) is training paradigm proposed in this study to optimize the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) in processing and leveraging our Instructional Linguistic 4 approach. CIT draws inspiration from the work of Lee et al. (2024) and Feng et al. (2023), who explored LLM training through curriculum learning, rubrics, and self-correction mechanisms. Unlike conventional fine-tuning, which typically involves single inputtarget pair, CIT is designed to continuously incorporate multiple instruction forms for the same input. Specifically, each input is associated with set of instructions I1, I2, I3, I4, all corresponding to the same translation target y. The training process is carried out sequentially, where the model is fine-tuned with the pairs (x, I1) y, followed by (x, I2) y, and so on, until (x, I4) y. Overview of the CIT-based fine-tuning process is illustrated in Figure 5. Figure 5: Continual Instruction Tuning adopts continual training paradigm in which the model learns iteratively, processing one data sample for every four instruction prompts.

sections.title: 6 Experimental Settings
sections.content: 6.1 Data Acquisition Parallel Data The parallel sentences from Indonesian to Kupang Malay were collected and processed from several sources, namely the Bhinneka Korpus (Lopo and Tanone, 2024), BibleNLP4, and The Language Archive5. total of 66,521 sentence pairs were compiled, with 53,217 used for training and 13,304 for testing. Since the translation direction in this study is from Indonesian to Kupang Malay, English sentences in BibleNLP and The Language Archive were first translated into Indonesian using the NLLB model6. To ensure translation quality, back-translation was performed and evaluated using SacreBLEU and BERTScore7. The statistics of the parallel data and the back-translation results are presented in Table 1. In addition, bilingual dictionary was compiled from Kamus Pengantar Bahasa Melayu Kupang by Jacob and Grimes (2003), consisting of approximately 3,200 lexical entries. Monolingual Data This study also employs monolingual data in Kupang Malay to support the development of FastText model, which plays crucial role in the instruction construction pro4https://github.com/BibleNLP/ebible 5https://archive.mpi.nl/tla/ 6https://huggingface.co/facebook/ nllb-200-distilled-1.3B 7https://huggingface.co/FacebookAI/ roberta-large Source # Sentences SacreBLEU BERTScore Parameter mT5 small mT5 base mT5 large Bible NLP Bhinneka Corpus The Language Archive Tapaleuk Total Parallel Data Train Test Total Instruction Data Train Test Experience Replay 1,229 4,000 56,740 4,552 66,521 53,217 13, 227,172 212,868 13,304 1,000 35.96 32.69 24.34 0.94 0.93 0.90 Table 1: Data statistics and back-translation evaluation. For test instruction data is randomly chosen over four instructional linguistic during testing. cess within the instructional linguistic approach. The monolingual dataset is collected from several primary sources in Kupang Malay, including Tapaleuk News, the Jakarta Field Station (Station, 19802014), Taxi1500-RawData (Ma et al., 2025), as well as collections of poetry (Manhitu, 2005b) and pantun8 (Manhitu, 2005a). The complete statistics of the monolingual dataset used are presented in Table 2. Dataset # of Texts Avg. Words Max. Words Min. Words Tapaleuk News Jakarta Field Station Taxi1500-RawData Poem & Pantun 99 66 18,452 37 704.42 6.24 43.31 56.02 1,078 13,184 284 181 227 879 1 18 Table 2: Statistics of monolingual dataset in Kupang Malay. 6.2 Backbone Models We utilize the Cendol mT5 model (Cahyawijaya et al., 2024), an open-source multilingual LLM supporting Indonesian and 18 local languages. three variants of the Cendol mT5 Specifically, model are employed; ranging from 350 and 580 million parameters to 1.2 billion parameters, respectively. To maintain models generalization, the Experience Replay technique (Rolnick et al., 2019) is applied. We used 1,000 additional training samples from the original Cendol models training collection9 and combined them with the constructed instruction data for training. In addition, due to computational and storage constraints, optimization strategies are applied. This 8A form of traditional Indonesian poetry 9https://huggingface.co/datasets/ indonlp/cendol_collection_v2 max_input_length max_output_length batch_size bfp16 lr 128 52 32 True 3e-5 128 52 16 True 3e128 52 16 True 3e-5 Table 3: Hyperparameter settings for Cendol mT5 models (small to large). including the use of Brain Floating Point 16-bit (BF16) for reduced precision computation, gradient checkpointing to store partial activations before the backward pass, and gradient accumulation over two steps. All training parameters are detailed in Table 3. 6.3 Computational Resources All experiments were conducted using single 40GB A100 GPU for all model training variations, and single 8GB GeForce RTX 4060 Ti for evaluation and additional inference resources. The training and evaluation processes took total of approximately 13 days, including two days each for training the small, base, and large model sizes. Additionally, the Weights & Biases platform was used to monitor model training and GPU utilization, providing visualization and model tracking capabilities. 6.4 Evaluation Suite Model Baseline The model baseline consists of comparisons between models trained with standard instructions and those with linguistic instructional prompts. The models using standard instructions were trained using the Cendol-mT5 model in small, base, and large parameter sizes. To ensure comparability, all model training parameters were kept consistent (3) and following the instruction template for the baseline model, adopted from Cahyawijaya et al. (2024) for translation tasks. Comparison with Other Models We conduct evaluation using both zero-shot and few-shot prompting on multilingual LLMs. Specifically, the models used include BLOOMZ-7B1-MT and mT0-XXL-MT (Muennighoff et al., 2023), Sailor-7B-Chat (Dou et al., 2024), Aya-Expanse8B (Dang et al., 2024), SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat (Zhang et al., 2024b), Cendol-LLaMA2-7b-Inst, and Cendol-mT5-XL-Inst (Cahyawijaya et al., Model SacreBLEU chrF++ TER ROUGE-L Standard Prompt Cendol-mT5-small-tuned Cendol-mT5-base-tuned Cendol-mT5-large-tuned 6.62 8.77 8.97 23.79 26.91 27.30 102.5 98.08 97.13 Independent Prompt Lius-Large-Contextual Lius-Large-Phonetic Lius-Large-List-Group-Label Lius-Large-Semantic 9.47 9.42 9.37 9. 27.78 27.63 27.85 27.70 96.90 97.52 96.41 96.83 Instructional Linguistic Prompt Lius-Small-MT Lius-Base-MT Lius-Large-MT 8.72 11.63 12.99 26.70 30.26 32. 97.96 92.90 90.36 19.97 25.23 25.94 26.55 26.48 26.86 26.55 24.99 29.75 31.55 Table 4: Performance comparison between Lius and Baseline Models. Ours is denoted as Instruction Linguistic Prompt, while Independent Prompt is independent instructional linguistic variants. 2024). Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, the only existing Kupang Malay translation models are the multilingual Madlad400-3BMT and Madlad400-7B-MT models by Google (Kudugunta et al., 2023). These models were trained on 100 billion sentences across 419 languages, including 25.4 thousand Kupang Malay sentences, all sourced from Bible texts. Additionally, Madlad400 requires language prefix <2trg> in the input sentence. Human Evaluation We conduct further evaluation by human evaluators to maintain the quality of translations. More specifically, evaluators were asked to rate the translation outputs using two evaluation metrics: adequacy and fluency. Each metric follows four-level scale and conducted by recruiting several native speakers to assess and rate the translation outputs. The rankings were analyzed using inter-annotator agreement, measured with the Kappa coefficient.

sections.title: 7 Result & Analysis
sections.content: 7.1 Comparison with Baseline Overall, the model performance across all experiments is shown in Table 4. Lius models achieved superior performance across all evaluation metrics, with the 1.2 billion parameters model demonstrating the highest average performance by 26 points on each metric and among the Lius variants. Notably, even the smaller variant with 300 million parameters, the model outperformed the standard instruction model of the same size by two points. This indicates that our approach remains effective even for smaller models in lowresource scenarios. In addition, all independent instructional outperformed the standard instruction models. For instance, SacreBLEU scores for these variants ranged from 9.21 to 9.47, surpassing the best-performing standard model. This highlights the benefit of providing task-specific instructions over generic templates. Semantic and Contextual Analysis Lius improving model performance by approximately 46 points across all evaluation metrics. This indicates that our approach enables the model to capture deeper semantic features and comprehend sentence context. For instance, the lower score on the TER metric suggests that fewer edits are required to match the models translation output with the reference. This can be proved as denoted in Figure 6b, which shows the relationship between SacreBLEU and chrF++ scores, where models with higher BLEU scores also tend to have higher chrF++ scores. (a) (b) Figure 6: (a) Lius performance and its independent instructional models using Large model. (b) Correlation between BLEU and chrF++ metrics. Independent Prompt Contribution We found that each independent instructional is slightly better than the standard baseline, which suggests that it contributes significantly to the performance of the Lius model. To better understand this finding, four example translations were selected and presented in Table 5. There are different variations across the outputs from each instruction type. For example, in the target sentence "Botong sangka dia pung badan baisi, tagal su ampa bulan sonde datang bulan", the Semantic and Phonetic instructions signal the word "kotong" (we), which semantically aligns with "botong" (we). Furthermore, Lius is also capable of capturing the semantic meaning of the sentence. For instance, the phrase "balakang" (restroom) is consistently translated as "kamar kici" (small room/restroom) in Kupang Malay. Target Adu, be pung paru saki! Be parmisi pi balakang do. Botong sangka dia pung badan baisi, tagal su ampa bulan sonde datang bulan. Context aduh! beta pung perut saket. Mamisi, beta mau pi kamar kecil dolo. Beta pikir dia hamil, karna su empat bulan sonde haid. Semantic aduh! beta pung perut saket. Mamisi, beta mau pi kamar kecil dolo. dia kotong hamil, su empat bulan dia son haid. kira karna Phonetic adu! beta pung perut saket. Mamisi, beta mau pi kamar kecil dolo. dia kotong hamil, su empat bulan dia tida haid. kira karna List-Group-Label aduh! beta pung perut saket banget. Maaf, beta mau pi kamar kecil dolo. Beta pikir dia hamil, karna su ampa bulan sonde haid. Table 5: Example of translation result across different instruction. Instructional Aduuu! Beta pung paru talalu saki. Tolong, beta pi kamar kici dolo. Kotong hamil, bulan son haid. dia te su ampat kira Training Process The training results of each model are illustrated in Figure 13. In general, models exhibit steady downward trend in loss, indicating that the models are effectively learning and improving their translations over time. Lius with large parameter size began with higher initial loss, however, as training progressed, it was able to significantly reduce its loss. In terms of convergence, the Small and Base models tended to converge faster than the Large model, primarily due to having fewer parameters to adjust. In addition, the computational cost and training efficiency of the Large model requires significantly more storage capacity as well as increased infrastructure during training. For instance, training duration increases exponentially, from 1 hour and 9 minutes to over 6 hours, reflecting rise in computational complexity. Table 6: Efficiency Metrics of Lius Model Variants Aspect Lius-Small-MT Lius-Base-MT Lius-Large-MT Parameters Storage (GB) Training Time (hh:mm:ss) Memory Usage (%) Inference Time (s) 350M 1.13 01:09:00 28 14.21 580M 1.95 03:22:00 39 22.24 1.2B 3.4 06:35:00 78 72.35 Copying Behaviour Furthermore, we investigated models tendency to directly copy tokens from the input sentence or Copying behaviour, which can degrades translation quality (Liu et al., 2021). We compute both copy accuracy and copy rate for each model, higher copy value indicates stronger tendency to replicate input tokens. We inspected that, both metrics exhibit downward trend as model size increases. For instance, Lius with small parameter size shows the highest copy accuracy at 23.62%, while the large one decreases to 20%. Similarly, the Copy Rate decreases from 23.54% in the Small model to 19.38% in the Large model. Furthermore, we found that the declining trend in both Copy Accuracy and Copy Rate in the Base Figure 7: Analysis of Copying Behaviour across Models and Large models implies that increased model capacity enables better comprehension of linguistic context, encouraging the model to translate rather than copy. For instance, as shown in Figure 7, words like "Presiden", "Jokowi", "2023", "Indonesia", and "Jakarta" are accurately copied by the model. Meanwhile, terms such as "tahun" are translated into "taon" and "sebagai" into "sabage", indicating direct translation by the model. 7.2 Existing NMT & LLMs Comparison We conduct comparison using Madlad400-3B-MT and Madlad400-7B-MT to measure the further capabilities of Lius. As shown in Table 7, the multilingual NMT models achieve substantially lower scores across all evaluation metrics, whereas the Lius model demonstrates up to sixfold improvement. This implies that although Madlad400 has larger number of parameters its performance in translating Kupang Malay text remains highly limited. Similarly, Lius model significantly outperforms regional LLMs across all evaluation metrics with significant margin on zero-shot and few-shot prompting. For instance, in few-shot scenario, multilingual models such as mT0-XXL-MT and BLOOMZ-7B1-MT relatively underperform, with SacreBLEU scores of only 2.15 and 1.13 respectively, and extremely high TER values (173.29 and 372.48). This indicates that internally, Lius has better semantic understanding, task awareness, and better alignment to translation tasks rather Model Zero-shot Few-shot SacreBLEU chrF++ TER ROUGEL SacreBLEU chrF++ TER ROUGEL BLOOMZ-7B1-MT mT0-XL mT0-XXL-MT Sailor-7B-Chat Aya-Expanse-8B SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat Cendol-LLaMA2-7b-inst Cendol-mT5-XL-Inst Madlad400-3B-MT Madlad400-7B-MT 3.14 4.25 4.86 3.47 3.35 4.44 1.87 3.72 2.22 2.22 Multilingual & Regional LLMs 15.03 21.00 22.93 22.36 23.81 21.80 11.98 20.68 148.08 130.35 114.60 154.16 178.34 146.95 122.50 143. 6.37 12.26 14.40 13.81 16.10 15.95 4.31 11.00 Multilingual NMT 20.96 20.36 136.81 128.79 13.94 13.32 Lius Models 1.13 2.25 2.15 3.47 1.12 1.45 13.17 19.19 18.31 22.36 10.88 5.96 372.48 163.03 173.29 154.16 294.83 134.57 4.69 12.07 11.11 13.81 4.46 2.23 Lius-Small-MT Lius-Base-MT Lius-Large-MT 9.20 11.88 13.27 27.75 31.23 32.80 98.76 92.83 89. 25.55 30.52 32.62 8.72 11.63 12.99 26.70 30.26 32.01 97.96 92.90 90.36 24.99 29.75 31.55 Table 7: Performance comparison of multilingual, regional LLM and NMT, and Lius models on zeroshot and few-shot prompting settings. There are two models excluded in Few-shot prompting due to limited computational time. than the other multilingual and regional LLMs. 7.3 Generalization Towards Unseen Data Unseen Language To evaluate multilingual capability of the model, we conduct an experiment involving Javanese, Sundanese, and Indonesian in bidirectional translation setup using 100 randomly sampled sentences. The models performance evaluation is shown in Figure 8. Surprisingly, the evaluation results indicate that the model retains strong multilingual signals. For example, translation from Javanese to Kupang Malay achieves SacreBLEU scores of 10.17, with chrF++ of 26.76 and ROUGE-L of 23.10. This result highlight the models generalization on cross-lingual transfer. Figure 8: Bidirectional translation performance of the Lius Model. Unseen Task In addition to its multilingual capability, we investigated the Lius multitasking ability. Based on the analysis presented in Table 8, the model demonstrates its multitasking capability despite being fine-tuned specifically for translation tasks. For instance, in the sentiment analysis task, the model consistently classifies text with "negative" sentiment, while in the topic modeling task, the model accurately categorizes the input as "sad". These indicate the models ability to capture cross-lingual emotional nuance in other languages, such as complex regional language and non-standard lexical variations. 7.4 Robustness Testing We conducted series of evaluations on the Lius model to assess its robustness under various perturbations applied to the input text. Overall, as shown in Figure 9, the model performs reasonably well on modified sentences, with the performance gap relatively small compared to the unmodified input. These results demonstrate the models ability to reconstruct structured and contextually appropriate sentences despite input variations. 7.5 Human Evaluation Quality Rating Distribution Human Evaluation is conducted by involving native speakers to assess the translation results of the model. In genTask Input Sentiment Analysis Question Answering - Does the text contain an element of surprise? Topic Modeling (fear, disgusted, sad, happy, angry, surprise, shame) mung kamare mambu rokok lan banyu kamar mburi trocoh neng kamar karo AC kurang adem. Ngapurane komplen supoyo dijogo kualitase sirah orok bijil simkuring nu isuk2 rek nyimpen pakean ka cucian jeung ninggali ti panto gudang (ieu sigana nganyimpang, simkuring soalna simkuring sieun pisan waktos eta) Alhamdulillah ammetai persib, tinroa tong sanrannui atia, nang. MVP anne bangngia anjo yang [USERNAME] pakullei acini persib na tena apau leba siallo susai akomunikasi kah kasibukanganna ade bedur Target negative Output negative no sad no sad Table 8: Examples of multitasking performance on the Lius Model. Figure 9: Comparison of model performance on perturbed inputs: Word Shuffling, Word deletion, and Typographical errors. eral, the average translation scores fall within the range of 3 to 4, presented in Figure 10b, indicating that the majority of the translation outputs are considered reasonably good in terms of both fluency and adequacy. However, the adequacy score distribution appears slightly more dispersed, suggesting that conveying meaning accurately in some translations remains challenge. Furthermore, some evaluators tend to consistently assign higher or lower score, may reflect subjectivity, and individual preferences. On the other hand, there is greater disagreement among raters in assessing sentence naturalness, as shown in Figures 10a, which the standard deviation of fluency ratings is generally higher than that of adequacy. Inter-annotator Agreement In addition to analyzing score distribution and inter-rater variabil- (a) (b) Figure 10: (a) Variability score for fluency and adequacy. (b) Distribution score for translation quality based on fluency and adequacy. ity, the ordinal Cohens Kappa coefficient was also calculated to evaluate the level of agreement among evaluators in scoring each translation output. To mitigate potential bias from evaluators who consistently assign overly high or low scores, score normalization was applied prior to the calculation. The results indicate that the weighted Kappa value for the adequacy is 0.2099, while for fluency it is 0.1339. According to the interpretation scale by Landis and Koch (1977), both values fall within the slight agreement category, suggesting low level of agreement among evaluators. We suspected that, internally, Kupang Malay does not yet have widely recognized standardized guideline, even though it has evolved into distinct language (Jacob and Grimes, 2003). This lack of standardization allows for divergent perceptions among speakers when evaluating translation quality.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: This study proposes method called the linguistic instructional approach to train LLMs based on the Continual Instruction Tuning paradigm. It incorporates specific instructions and enables iterative instruction-based during training process. The model is trained using the Cendol-mT5 architecture and demonstrates superior performance compared to models trained with standard instructions, outperforming both NMT models and multilingual LLMs in zero-shot and few-shot prompting scenarios, with performance gaps ranging from 10 to 13 points across various metrics. The model also retains its multilingual capabilities and performs competently on other tasks such as sentiment analysis and question answering. human evaluation was also conducted, showing that the model still fall short of high-quality standards as judged by native speakers. The lack of standardized guideline for Kupang Malay poses considerable challenge in the human evaluation process.

sections.title: A Robustness Testing Examples
sections.content: Typographical errors As shown in Table 9, words such as "dan" are altered to "daan", "bajunya" to "bjunya", and "kemudian" to "emudian". Nevertheless, the model is capable of capturing the intended meaning from such noisy input. In some cases, the model successfully maps distorted words to semantically relevant expressions, such as transforming "emudian" into "abis itu" in Kupang Malay. This demonstrates the models capacity to understand and reconstruct meaning despite distortions introduced by typographical noise. Original Text Typos Output Setelah itu, baptua melipat koran dan kemudian menyimpannya di dalam bajunya kemudian pulang. Setelah itu, baptua melipat koran daan kemudian menyimpannya di dalam bjunya emudian pulang. Abis itu, baptua lipat surat ko simpan di dia pung buk itu abis pulang. Table 9: Examples of Translations with Typographical Errors Word deletion As shown in Table 10, the phrase "kau akan mendapatkan tinjuku" is reduced to "kau tinjuku", which drastically alters the intended meaning. The output generated after word deletion reveals that the model struggles with such distortions, occasionally inserting unrelated elements such as bini and matua. This illustrates the models limitations in grasping holistic meaning when faced with incomplete input, leading to outputs that are not only inaccurate but also enriched with hallucinated content. Original Text Deletion Output Biarkan dia pergi atau kau tinjuku, Ama Jola ingin melompat pada tua. itu ko su Biar jalan lu puku be pung bini, Ama Jola mo malompa sang matua Biarkan dia pergi atau kau akan mendapatkan tinjuku, Jola Ama sudah ingin melompat pada tua. orang Table 10: Examples of Translations with Word Deletion Word Shuffling As shown in Table 11, where the word "pada" is randomly misplaced. Nevertheless, the model demonstrates the ability to reconstruct structured and contextually appropriate sentences. Although the translation of the word "bagitu" is inaccurate, the model still manages to produce diverse outputs that reflect resilience to structural perturbations in the input. Original Text Shuffled Output tapi aku tidak berpikir untuk menikah pada saat itu. tapi aku tidak berpikir pada menikah untuk saat itu. tapi itu waktu be son berpikir untuk menika bagitu. Table 11: Examples of Translations with Shuffled Input

sections.title: B Human Evaluation Process
sections.content: In your opinion, Human evaluation was conducted by involving native speakers to assess the translations produced by the Lius model. We evaluated based on two main is the grammar, questions: spelling, and sentence structure of the translation correct?which corresponds to the fluency metric; and In your opinion, has all the information in the source text been accurately translated?which corresponds to the adequacy metric. The evaluation took place over approximately two months, from January 21 to March 21, 2025. The process was carried out through web-based annotation platform built using the Python programming language10 and the Flask framework11. The user interface of the annotation platform is shown in Figure 11. Table 12: Evaluation Scale for Adequacy and Fluency Score Adequacy Fluency 4 3 2 1 All meaning preserved Most meaning preserved Some meaning preserved Moderately fluent Little meaning preserved Very fluent Fluent Not fluent The evaluators came from diverse backgrounds, lecturers, high including university students, 10https://www.python.org/ 11https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/ stable/ Figure 11: User Interface of Annotation Application school teachers, civil servants, and government language development officials. They were mostly residents of Kupang Malay-speaking areas, such as the cities of Kupang, SoE, and parts of Sabu Island. Of the 40 respondents registered in the system, 18 successfully completed the full evaluation. Four evaluators were excluded due to providing identical scores across all translation samples. Examples of the evaluation results for input, translation, and their corresponding fluency and adequacy scores are presented in Table 13.

sections.title: C Instruction Template
sections.content: The instruction template refers to direct commands used for translation tasks and constitutes core component of the prompt template, marked with the label INSTRUCTION. total of nine types of instruction template variations were deincluding styles such as formal-direct veloped, commands, direct questions, and narrative-style prompts. Each variation consists of 3 to 5 instructions, resulting in total of 50 distinct instructions. Examples of these instruction template variations are shown in Table 14. The INPUT represents the sentence to be translated, SOURCE denotes the source language (Indonesian), and TARGET refers to the target language (English). For each data instance, the selection of an instruction template is performed randomly. Examples of single input data entries for each type of instruction can be seen in Table 15, Table 16, Table 17, and Table 18. For instance, in the case of the List-Group-Label-based instruction, the sentence Aku makan kue tiga potong. serves as the input text, while senkala, siri, tences such as Label 1: ID Input Text Translation Output Fluency Adequacy 4986 Tolong kalian pikirkan sendiri. Ibu merasa sakit, hendak melahirkan 4987 4988 Saya belum begitu paham dengan maksudnya. Bisakah engkau menerangkannya kembali kepada saya? 4989 Anak kecil harus tidur lebih awal. 4990 4991 He! Jangan lambat seperti pengantin. Nanti kita ketinggalan kendaraan. Akhirnya kita hanya duduk termangu-mangu saja. 4992 Waktu dia baru tiba dari Jakarta, dia pergi ke Kolhua untuk menegur-sapa pamannya Jangan duduk mengangkang. Nanti anak laki-laki bisa melihat celana dalammu. 4993 4995 Marilah kita bermain sembunyi, yuk? Engkau yang mencari dan aku yang bersembunyi, ya? Pikir sa. Mana yang lebe bae? Kotong iko bapa-bapa pung parenta, ko kotong iko Tuhan pung parenta? kuda pung ekor Beta balóm mangarti deng dia pung maksud bagini. Bosong bisa carita lai sang beta? ana kici harus tidor dolo. mama su sakit mau melahirkan... He! Jang lambat sama ke pengantin, nanti kotong katinggal kendaraan, akirnya kotong dudu raguragu sa. baru datang dari Jakarta dia pi Kolhua untuk tegur dia pung om. Jalan capat! Jang dudu mangangkang, nanti ana laki laki liat lu pung calana dalam. Mari kotong maen sembunyi, a?
